landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: His death at 96 killed his brother Samuel
narrative_blocks.body: On February 19, 1787, Giles Hooker died in Farmington at age 96. When the news reached his older brother Samuel — then age 98 in Kensington — Samuel "fell back in his chair and expired" on March 1, 1787. Two brothers, together accounting for nearly 195 years of life, died within ten days of each other.

narrative_blocks.header: The well stone on Main Street
narrative_blocks.body: He and Martha lived on the George Cowles place on Main Street, Farmington. In Edward Hooker's time (1909), a flat stone in the sidewalk just below the brow of the hill still covered the old well of the Giles Hooker house. Directly opposite, his father Rev. Samuel Hooker had lived in the Mix House — just south of the old burying ground.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.body: On February 19, 1787, Giles Hooker died in Farmington at age 96. When the news reached his older brother Samuel — then age 98 in Kensington — Samuel "fell back in his chair and expired" on March 1, 1787. Two brothers, together accounting for nearly 195 years of life, died within ten days of each other.

narrative_blocks.header: Her grandfather was Secretary of State
narrative_blocks.body: Her maternal grandfather was Hon. John Allyn (Allen), Secretary of State for the Colony of Connecticut. Her father, Capt. Aaron Cooke IV, served as Hartford's deputy to the Connecticut General Assembly for fifteen sessions and chaired the committee to distribute the new laws in 1702.

cross_connections.display_label: Her second cousin Abigail married her husband's younger brother James.

sources.short: Genealogy of the Cowles family in America (Cowles, 1929)
Memorial History of Hartford County, Connecticut (Trumbull ed., 1886) — Unionville chapter by James L. Cowles

career.title: Selectman of Farmington
career.notes: Documented as Selectman of Farmington on January 10, 1816 when he co-signed the certificate of examination for the manumission of Titus, slave of Capt. Joshua Youngs (alongside Justice of the Peace John Mix and fellow Selectman Samuel Richards). Per Trumbull's Memorial History of Hartford County (1886), Unionville chapter by James L. Cowles.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Tunxis-related officials in early 1800s Farmington.
Revolutionary grandfather; she pioneered California through her marriage.
Both Hooker descendants involved with Tunxis affairs, 1738 and 1804.

military_service.notes: Battle of Long Island Jul 1776; Saratoga and SURRENDER OF BURGOYNE Oct 17, 1777; under Col. Noadiah Hooker 1779; Sergeant-Major 1779; clerk to Lt.-Col. Austin 1781. Maj.-Gen. Parsons recommended him to Washington for commission, May 20, 1782 (Library of Congress, Washington Papers, Vol.3, p.2122). Pensioned 1832.

Revolutionary War service per pension application: July 1776 under Capt. John Porter to NY (in Battle of Long Island, retreated to Harlem Plains and over King's Bridge to White Plains; regiment disbanded Sept 28, 1776); 1777 under Capt. Ichabod Norton (attacked fort at Kingsbridge, defeated, discharged); September 1777 joined Capt. Ichabod Norton commanding regt. of 4 cos. 15th Conn. Militia, proceeded to Saratoga, present at the decisive action of Oct 7 and at the surrender of Burgoyne Oct 17, 1777; 1779 under Capt. Elisha Scott Col. Noadiah Hooker to New Haven; served as Sergeant-Major under Major Ichabod Norton; September 1781 drafted for three-week tour to New Haven in Lt-Col. Aaron Austin's regt., taken into Col. Austin's family as clerk/secretary. Pensioned under Act of June 7, 1832 commencing March 4, 1831, paid to death August 4, 1850.

documents.document_blurb: 1804 Connecticut General Assembly Resolve appointing Ezekiel Cowles Jr. as Overseer of the Farmington Tunxis Indians (replacing Capt. John Porter).

career.title: Member, Connecticut State Legislature
career.notes: Represented Farmington in the State Legislature.

career.title: Overseer of the Tunxis Indians
career.notes: Appointed by the Connecticut Legislature in 1804 as overseer and advisor of the Tunxis (Farmington Indians), replacing Capt. John Porter (under whom Ezekiel had served in 1776). The position made him one of a series of state-appointed Anglo-American managers of what remained of the Tunxis people's affairs and lands during a period of dispossession.

career.title: Farmer
career.notes: Farmer at Farmington Connecticut throughout adult life.

narrative_blocks.header: Recommended to Washington for a commission
narrative_blocks.body: On May 20, 1782 Maj.-Gen. Samuel Holden Parsons wrote to General Washington recommending Sergeant-Major Ezekiel Cowles for a subaltern commission. Ezekiel had been at Long Island, Harlem Plains, and the surrender of Burgoyne; the war ended before the commission could be granted. The Parsons letter is preserved at the Library of Congress.

narrative_blocks.header: Appointed overseer of the Tunxis in 1804
narrative_blocks.body: In 1804 the Connecticut General Assembly appointed Ezekiel Cowles Jr. overseer and advisor of the Tunxis Indians at Farmington, replacing Capt. John Porter — under whom Ezekiel had marched to the Battle of Long Island in 1776. The role placed him in the same Tunxis-management chain as Justice John Mix, who signed the 1802 Sue Squa land deed and the 1816 Titus emancipation.

narrative_blocks.header: Four overlapping offices across twenty-five years
narrative_blocks.body: Ezekiel filled four overlapping public offices across twenty-five years in Farmington: State Tax Collector (1794-1798), Deputy Sheriff (1802-1814), State Legislator (1807-1810), and Justice of the Peace (1809-1818). His 1850 estate inventoried at $6,549.12 — a substantial farmer's wealth in mid-century Connecticut.

cross_connections.display_label: He studied theology under Jonathan Edwards the Younger in New Haven.
Both Hooker descendants built the Connecticut Western Reserve.
She rode 600 miles in 1810 to recruit him to Ohio
Co-founders of Austinburg church and Western Reserve College

burial.plot_notes: Buried beside his wife Sallie White Cowles and son Edward. Funeral held in the church he helped build.

institutions.institution_blurb: Graduated Yale College 1789 with honors; D.D. awarded by Williams College 1823.
One of the original founders of Western Reserve College (now Case Western Reserve University) in 1826; drove the founding stake on College Hill, Hudson Ohio.
Ordained minister at First Congregational Church, Bristol CT 1792-1810.

narrative_blocks.header: A thirty-day wagon journey from Bristol
narrative_blocks.body: In 1811 Cowles loaded his family of nine children onto two wagons and traveled thirty to forty days over mud roads to Austinburg, Ohio — a journey so final that returning was considered nearly impossible. His congregation built the first church with a steeple in the state of Ohio, and he served it for nearly twenty-five years.

narrative_blocks.header: Drove the founding stake of Ohio's first college
narrative_blocks.body: In 1825 Cowles helped found Western Reserve College — called 'Yale of the West' for sharing Yale's motto, standards, and curriculum. He was among those who drove a stake on College Hill in Hudson, Ohio, and his name appears as an original incorporator. The college is now Case Western Reserve University.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1789, then theology with Edwards the Younger
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale 1789 Cowles read theology with tutor William Robinson before continuing under Rev. Dr. Jonathan Edwards Jr. at New Haven, son of the Great Awakening preacher. Edwards Jr. ordained him at Bristol's First Church on October 17, 1792.

narrative_blocks.header: 'A year never to be forgotten' — 1799
narrative_blocks.body: A revival swept his Bristol parish in 1799 and added 218 members to the church, the harvest of his early ministry. Three years later he was preaching publicly against the Bristol Baptists in defense of infant baptism, sermons that drew fierce reply.

narrative_blocks.header: His Austinburg parsonage gathered the era's reformers
narrative_blocks.body: His 320-acre Austinburg parsonage built in 1815 housed the Western Reserve's largest county library and a steady traffic of visitors — Bishop Philander Chase passing through on his Ohio diocese rounds, anti-slavery orators stopping for hospitality. The Cowles household became a way-station for the reform circuits of the Reserve.

narrative_blocks.header: Resigned the pulpit 1830, died still preaching
narrative_blocks.body: Cowles resigned his Austinburg pulpit in 1830 with a farewell sermon on 1 Samuel 12:23: 'God forbid that I should sin against the Lord in ceasing to pray for you.' Five years later, on Sunday July 5, 1835, he died at his parsonage and the Western Reserve's clergy gathered to bury him in his own churchyard.

career.title: Congregational Minister
career.notes: Ordained Oct 17, 1792. Bristol CT 1792-1810. Austinburg OH 1811-1835.

career.title: Theological Student under Rev. Dr. Jonathan Edwards Jr.
career.notes: Read theology under Edwards the Younger after Yale graduation; tutored earlier under William Robinson.

career.title: Pastor
career.organization: First Congregational Church of Bristol (New Cambridge)
career.notes: Ordained October 17, 1792. Dismissed May 24, 1810 in preparation for Ohio migration.

career.title: Pastor
career.organization: United Church of Austinburg and Morgan
career.notes: Installed September 25, 1811. Built first steepled church in Ohio. Resigned pulpit 1830.

career.title: Co-founder
career.notes: Original incorporator; drove the founding stake on College Hill, Hudson Ohio. Now Case Western Reserve University.

education.notes: Prepared under Rev. Wm. Robinson of Southington; entered Yale Jun 1, 1786; graduated Sep 9, 1789.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.photo_notes: Illustration of the Cowles family residence, Austinburg, Ohio (1878). No portrait survives.

sources.notes: Samuel Hooker Jr. biography; confirms Plymouth birth, Hartford merchant activity

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Plymouth in his father's transition year
narrative_blocks.body: He was born in Plymouth, Massachusetts on May 29, 1661 — where his father Rev. Samuel Hooker had been preaching for several years. The family moved to Farmington that same year, where his father succeeded his brother-in-law Roger Newton as minister.

narrative_blocks.header: His house stood south of the burial ground
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Hooker II had a fine residence at Farmington, next south of the old burial ground — later known as the Mix house — and ran a store for mercantile business in Hartford. He was one of the first of the Farmington Hookers to move from ministry into trade.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a wealthy sea captain's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: In 1687 Samuel Hooker married Mehitabel Hamlin of Middletown, daughter of Capt. Giles Hamlin, a noted and wealthy sea captain and merchant. Hooker kept a store and traded out of Hartford while living at Farmington.

career.notes: Mercantile business. Store and fine residence at Farmington.

education.notes: B.A. Harvard; later a fellow of the college. (Julius Gay, Farmington Historical Pamphlets)

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Burying Ground, Hartford CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of a sea captain
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Giles Hamlin was a noted and wealthy sea captain and merchant of Middletown, Connecticut. Her mother Esther Crow was the daughter of John Crow, merchant of Hartford — placing Mehitabel at the intersection of two of colonial Connecticut's most prominent merchant families.

bio.bio_blurb: Daughter of Middletown sea captain Giles Hamlin

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Burying Ground, Hartford CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

No stone survives. Well-attested tradition places the grave at the south side of Rev. Samuel Whitman's marked grave. "Mr. Whitman like Mr. Hooker, died while the minister of the town, and tradition says he was buried by the side of Mr. Hooker."

sources.notes: John Winthrop Jr., Alchemy and the Creation of New England Culture, 1606-1676 (Homer Babbidge Prize)

narrative_blocks.header: He inherited his father's library — and his calling
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's will gave his library to John on condition that John pursue ministry; if John faltered, the entire library and manuscripts went to Samuel. John did not become a minister. Samuel received his father's books — and then dedicated his own life to the ministry his father had hoped for.

narrative_blocks.header: Prosecutor in the Hartford witch trials
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1660s he formed a prosecutorial tribunal with Rev. Samuel Stone of Hartford and Rev. Joseph Haynes of Wethersfield. Connecticut tried witches more aggressively than Massachusetts in this decade — three decades before the Salem trials.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordered to King Philip's War, then quietly replaced
narrative_blocks.body: On March 9, 1675-6, the Council ordered Hooker to prepare to march into King Philip's War with Major Treat's army toward Hadley and Northampton. Two days later, the Council quietly replaced him with Rev. Israel Chauncy. No reason was recorded — only the substitution.

narrative_blocks.header: He persuaded Farmington to found a free school
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1685, the town of Farmington voted to give £30 for a schoolmaster for one year — and declared it a free school for the town, almost certainly at Hooker's instigation. It was among the earliest free public schools in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: The 'Fervent Hooker,' who preached from the heart
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Pitkin described him as 'an excellent preacher, his composition good, his address pathetic, warm and engaging.' Hooker told a friend he had three things to do with his sermons before delivering them: write them, commit them to memory, and get them into his heart.

cross_connections.display_label: Five Hooker generations on the same Farmington ground.
Farmington's first pastor, whom Hooker succeeded
His grandfather Tobie Willet sailed with John Talcott on the Lion.
Her husband's Prudden line connects to his wife Mary Willet's family
was his father-in-law; First Mayor of New York City, 1665

quotes.text: Thus we have to this day among us our dead Hooker, yet living in his worthy son Samuel Hooker, an able, faithful, useful minister at Farmington, in the Colony of Connecticut.

education.notes: B.A. 1653. Entered Harvard 1651. Chosen Fellow of the College November 27, 1654.

burial.plot_notes: Near center of burying ground, near East Norwalk railway station

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of New York City's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City in 1665, when the English renamed the Dutch colony. He had previously succeeded Capt. Miles Standish as commander of Plymouth's militia — one of the most storied military commands in early New England. Mary carried this distinguished lineage to Farmington when she married Rev. Samuel Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of Willet women known for beauty
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was described as possessing 'beauty and majesty almost divine.' A portrait of Mary's daughter Mary Hooker Pierpont survives, and from it contemporaries formed 'the opinion that she was a woman of uncommon beauty.' Her daughter Sarah was 'accounted the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.'

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at 66 — outlived two minister husbands
narrative_blocks.body: After Samuel Hooker's death in 1697, Mary married Reverend Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook at age 66. When he died in 1709 she moved to Norwalk to live with her son-in-law Reverend Stephen Buckingham, where she remained until her death in 1712 at age 74.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her daughter married father and son
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died, Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook in 1703. Her daughter Sarah was the wife of his son, Rev. Stephen Buckingham, so a mother and daughter had married a father and his son.

cross_connections.display_label: Her grandfather Tobie Willet sailed to New England with John Talcott.
Her father Thomas Willet's widow's first husband was ancestor of H00153's husband
